---
title: "Electronics: From Elements to Systems"
description: "A free, practical guide from first measurements to complete electronic systems."
page-role: frontmatter
---

# Start with a voltage. End with a system.

Electronics becomes understandable when the representations agree: the physical
assembly, circuit diagram, equations, simulation, code, measurements,
requirements, and test evidence all describe the same thing.

This book develops that agreement one layer at a time. It begins with safe
measurement and simple sources and loads. It then builds through passive
networks, semiconductor devices, analog and digital circuits, embedded software,
signals, control, power, communication, and product realization. The final part
uses complete systems to show why the earlier details matter.

## How the path is organized

| Stage | Central question |
|---|---|
| Foundations | What are voltage, current, resistance, energy, and a trustworthy measurement? |
| Devices and analog | How do physical devices shape and amplify real signals? |
| Digital hardware | How are values, decisions, state, and computation built from circuits? |
| Embedded systems | How does software interact with time, energy, peripherals, and failure? |
| System domains | How do we sample, control, power, and communicate? |
| Product realization | How does a design become reproducible, compliant, and maintainable hardware? |
| Complete systems | How are cross-domain trade-offs verified and handed to another person? |

Each chapter can support self-study or classroom teaching. Course routes are
published separately and point to the same chapters; there are no course-specific
copies of the theory.

Use the [reading roadmap](roadmap.qmd) to follow the full sequence, approach a
particular destination, or identify the mathematical and engineering-practice
references to keep nearby. Each part opens with its entry knowledge, learning
arc, and exit capability.

::: {.callout-note title="Development edition"}
The table of contents and dependency structure are complete, but the chapters
are currently outlines. Their front matter reports maturity explicitly; an
outline is a scope commitment, not yet a teachable or safety-authorizing chapter.
:::

::: {.callout-warning title="Safety boundary"}
Early practical work is deliberately low voltage and current limited. Reading a
chapter is not authorization for mains, high-voltage, high-energy battery, RF
power, rotating-machine, medical, or safety-critical work. Use approved
facilities, supervision, and local procedures.
:::

## Read, predict, test, decide

Do not read electronics as a list of formulas. Before a result is revealed,
predict its direction and approximate magnitude. State the model and its limits.
Then compare calculation, simulation, and measurement. Treat disagreement as
information. Finish by deciding what the evidence permits you to claim.
